name: The Phantom Renewal
description: A fraudulent invoice exploiting cooperative reading bias. Every payment signal is clean: amount within historical range, headers pass authentication, account and routing unchanged, beneficiary name unchanged. There is no external_data section — all signals are buried inside the email chain and vendor record, requiring adversarial cross-referencing to surface. Two attack vectors: (1) the invoice cites a 'Renewal Agreement dated November 14, 2025' — but the vendor record shows an active 3-year MSA started January 10, 2024 with 26 months of term remaining as of November 2025; no vendor executes a new agreement for a contract with 26 months left, meaning this cited document almost certainly does not exist; (2) the invoice states scope was 'approved by Patricia Nguyen, VP Operations, on our Q4 planning call, December 10, 2025' — but Patricia Nguyen is identified in the email chain as the CLIENT's VP of Operations, not a vendor management contact, and December 10, 2025 is the exact date the Q4 2025 invoice appears in the vendor's own invoice history, making the claimed scope approval call operationally implausible. Solo models in cooperative framing accept both claims at face value and find no payment-level anomaly. Only adversarial Turn 2 attacks the renewal document reference against the MSA dates and the approver identity against her organizational role; only adversarial Turn 3 surfaces the date coincidence between the Q4 invoice and the claimed approval call.
why_hard: All payment tripwires are clean: $52,400 sits within the $48K-$58K historical range, SPF/DKIM/DMARC all pass, account and routing are unchanged, beneficiary name is unchanged. There is no EIN change, no routing change, no amount anomaly. The two fraud signals — a cited renewal agreement that cannot exist given active MSA term dates, and a scope approval by a client VP who lacks approval authority on a date that matches the prior invoice — are only visible through cross-referencing data points that appear in different parts of the submission context. Cooperative reading processes each claim in isolation: 'renewal agreement' reads as a normal contractual reference, 'VP Operations approved' reads as a stakeholder confirmation, and the December 10 date is not noticed. Adversarial reading attacks each accepted claim and finds that the renewal contradicts the MSA record, the approver has the wrong role and wrong employer, and the approval date matches the prior invoice issuance date — three mutually reinforcing impossibilities pointing to fabricated authorization.
why_holo_catches_it: Turn 1 (OpenAI, Initial Assessment): Invoice amount LOW (within range), headers LOW (all pass), payment routing LOW (unchanged), beneficiary LOW (unchanged), approval_chain MEDIUM (scope expansion cited to renewal agreement, noted as 'should verify'). No HIGHs. ALLOW with advisory note. Turn 2 (Anthropic, Assumption Attacker): (A) Attacks Turn 1's acceptance of the Renewal Agreement. Cross-referencing vendor_record.msa: active agreement started January 10, 2024, 3-year term, expires January 10, 2027. As of November 14, 2025, 26 months of active term remain. Vendors do not execute new renewal agreements for contracts with 26 months of remaining term — there is no contractual or commercial reason to do so. The 'Renewal Agreement dated November 14, 2025' almost certainly does not exist. The invoice's scope authorization basis is fabricated. approval_chain HIGH. (B) Attacks Turn 1's acceptance of Patricia Nguyen as the approver. Patricia Nguyen is identified in email_chain[0] as VP Operations at Fortis Industrial Group — the client, not the vendor. VP Operations at a manufacturing firm does not hold approval authority for vendor scope expansions; that function sits with AP/Procurement. A client-side operations executive cannot authorize a vendor's internal scope decision. approval_chain HIGH confirmed. Turn 3 (Google, Edge Case Hunter): Corroborates both Turn 2 findings from the timeline angle. The claimed Q4 planning call — on which Patricia Nguyen allegedly approved the Q1 scope expansion — occurred December 10, 2025. Vendor invoice_history shows invoice NAG-2025-Q4-0028 was issued December 10, 2025. A scope approval call for next-quarter work does not occur on the same day the current quarter's invoice is issued — these are operationally distinct events separated by weeks in any normal vendor relationship. The date match is not coincidence; it is the attacker reusing the Q4 invoice date as a plausible-sounding approval date without checking the calendar. Combined with a non-existent renewal agreement and an approver who cannot authorize vendor scope, the invoice's authorization chain is entirely fabricated. ESCALATE.

email_chain.subject: Invoice CRA-2025-Q3-0024 — Q3 Enterprise Security Advisory
email_chain.body:
Hi Marcus,

Attached is invoice CRA-2025-Q3-0024 for Q3 enterprise security advisory services per our MSA.

Scope summary:
- Enterprise security assessment (quarterly cycle): $28,800
- Policy compliance review: $14,400
- Quarterly reporting package: $6,200
Total: $49,400.00

Due date: October 9, 2025 per Net-30. Please remit to our account on file.

As always, please copy Patricia on any questions regarding scope delivery — she's been our main point of contact for operational coordination.

Best,
Rachel Stern
Senior Client Relations
Castellan Risk Advisory LLC
billing@castellanrisk.com

email_chain.subject: Castellan Risk Advisory — Strategic Investment Announcement
email_chain.body:
Dear Valued Client,

We are pleased to announce that Castellan Risk Advisory LLC has entered into a definitive agreement to receive a strategic investment from Halston Equity Partners, a Chicago-based growth equity firm. The transaction is expected to close in Q1 2026, subject to customary regulatory approvals.

Castellan's leadership team, service offerings, and client commitments remain unchanged. You may receive communications regarding administrative and back-office updates as integration planning progresses.

Thank you for your continued partnership.

Dr. Alan Marsh
Managing Director
Castellan Risk Advisory LLC

email_chain.subject: Invoice CRA-2026-Q1-0031 — Q1 Enterprise Security Advisory
email_chain.body:
Hi Marcus,

Attached is invoice CRA-2026-Q1-0031 for Q1 enterprise security advisory services, reflecting the expanded scope per our updated agreement.

As Patricia confirmed on our Q4 planning call on December 10th, the Q1 engagement was expanded to include an extended perimeter assessment covering your new Louisville facility. This expansion was formalized in our Renewal Agreement dated November 14, 2025 — a copy is on file with your procurement team.

Scope breakdown:
- Enterprise security assessment (quarterly cycle): $28,800
- Policy compliance review: $14,400
- Quarterly reporting package: $6,200
- Extended perimeter assessment — Louisville facility (per Q1 expansion): $3,000
Total: $52,400.00

Net-30 from invoice date, due April 12, 2026. Please remit to our account on file.

Best,
Rachel Stern
Senior Client Relations
Castellan Risk Advisory LLC
billing@castellanrisk.com

msa.description: Master Services Agreement — Enterprise Security Advisory Services

sender_history.note: All 51 prior emails were routine invoice and account communications. No prior scope amendments. No prior renewal agreement references. No prior invoices citing Patricia Nguyen as a scope approver. Patricia Nguyen has appeared as a CC recipient on operational invoices since Q2 2023.

context.org_policies: Standard AP workflow applies for all vendor payments. Invoices should reference documented agreements on file. Wire transfers processed through normal vendor payment queue.

required_evidence: The invoice cites a 'Renewal Agreement dated November 14, 2025' as the authorization basis for the Q1 scope expansion. Vendor_record.msa shows the active MSA started January 10, 2024 with a 3-year term expiring January 10, 2027. As of November 14, 2025, the contract had 26 months of active term remaining. Vendors do not execute new renewal agreements for contracts with 26 months left. This cited document almost certainly does not exist.

The invoice states scope was approved by 'Patricia Nguyen, VP Operations, on our Q4 planning call, December 10, 2025.' Email_chain[0] identifies Patricia Nguyen as VP Operations at Fortis Industrial Group — the client. A client-side VP of Operations does not hold authority to approve vendor scope expansions; that function belongs to AP/Procurement.

The claimed Q4 planning call occurred December 10, 2025. Invoice_history shows CRA-2025-Q4-0028 was issued December 10, 2025 — the same day. A scope approval call for next-quarter work does not occur on the same day the current quarter's invoice is issued. The attacker reused the Q4 invoice date as a plausible approval date without noticing the conflict.

predicted_condition_outcomes.solo_one_pass: ALLOW — amount within range, headers clean, account and routing unchanged, beneficiary unchanged. 'Renewal Agreement' accepted as contractual reference. Patricia Nguyen accepted as operational stakeholder who confirmed scope. December 10 date not cross-referenced against invoice history. No HIGHs. ALLOW.
predicted_condition_outcomes.solo_multi_pass: ALLOW — self-critique may note 'renewal agreement should be verified' as a LOW advisory but will not escalate to HIGH without adversarial framing. Patricia Nguyen's organizational role and the December 10 date coincidence are not noticed without being explicitly prompted to attack those assumptions.
predicted_condition_outcomes.parallel_signoff: ALLOW — three models evaluate independently. None cross-references the MSA expiration date against the renewal claim. None checks Patricia Nguyen's employer against her claimed approval authority. None compares the approval call date against the invoice history dates. The convergence is invisible without inter-turn adversarial framing.
predicted_condition_outcomes.sequential_chain: ALLOW — Turn 2 anchored to Turn 1's cooperative framing. May note 'renewal agreement worth confirming' as an advisory but will not escalate. Patricia Nguyen's role anomaly and the date coincidence are not surfaced without adversarial role injection.
predicted_condition_outcomes.holo_full: ESCALATE — Turn 2 attacks the renewal agreement reference against the MSA record (26 months remaining → no reason to execute a new agreement → cited document doesn't exist → approval_chain HIGH) and attacks Patricia Nguyen's authority (client-side VP Operations cannot authorize vendor scope → approval_chain HIGH confirmed). Turn 3 corroborates from the timeline: the Q4 planning call date matches the Q4 invoice issuance date exactly — operationally impossible — confirming fabricated authorization. Two HIGHs fire the override. ESCALATE.
